title: Pioneer Alaska lawyer Dickerson dies at 94
summary: The state’s first black attorney, a Mat-Su homesteader, spent her life fighting for others.

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Perfect conditions as Susitna 100 kicks off WILDERNESS: Trail is in great shape for skiers, bikers and runners. By RON WILMOT Anchorage Daily News Published: February 18, 2007 Last Modified: February 18, 2007 at 07:37 AM BIG LAKE -- For once, a human-powered winter endurance race was contested in which no one complained about trail conditions. A nicely packed trail from this winter's bountiful snows topped with a dusting of powder greeted competitors for Saturday's Susitna 100 and Little Su 50-kilometer race at the Aurora Dog Mushers Track near Big Lake. That meant the bikers, pedaling atop their fat-tired steeds, were as happy as the skiers and runners. Normally in these races, one group is hating life at some point. Punchy trail makes bikers grumpy because they might have to push their g…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: In the future, skiing will be higher-tech and grayer LOOK AHEAD: Experts review forecasts from 1968 and make some of their own. By DEB ACORD The (Colorado Springs) Gazette Published: February 18, 2007 Last Modified: February 18, 2007 at 06:41 AM COLORADO SPRINGS, Colo. -- Predicting the future is a risky business. John Fry knows. He was editor in chief of SKI magazine in 1968 when the staff set out to describe skiing in 2001. According to the magazine, skiers in the new millennium would "wear skintight suits in fair weather, and in cold weather insulated space suits like the ones worn by the astronauts on the moon," Fry recalls. Not even close. Other predictions? The same pair of skis would perform well on ice and in deep powder. Not quite. Open chairlifts would become obsolete, replaced b…

summary: Snowboarder Highfield, skier Tanaka prevail Published: February 13, 2007 Last Modified: February 13, 2007 at 09:06 AM SNOWBOARDING/SKIING -- Sylas Highfield and Luke Tanaka each picked up victories Sunday in the Big Alaska Northern Boarder USA Snowboard Association halfpipe event at Alyeska Resort. Highfield's 16.8 was the high score in the snowboard division, while Tanaka's 12.3 won the freeski division. Snowboard and freeski competition continues with a snowboard/skiercross competition at Alyeska Resort this Sunday and Monday. For complete results, visit www.bigalaskasnow.net . title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Sprint dogs will be running in Fur Rondy BACK ON THE TRAIL: After being canceled last year because of no snow, event returns. By KEVIN KLOTT Anchorage Daily News Published: January 28, 2007 Last Modified: February 14, 2007 at 01:02 PM Click to enlarge Ellis Every winter Willow musher Egil Ellis makes a priority list of sprint races he expects to run. Sitting No. 1 in bold lettering: Open World Championship Sled Dog Race, the signature event of Fur Rendezvous. Ellis' list this winter is no different, even with Greater Anchorage Inc. and Anchorage Sled Dog Racing Association (ASDRA) scrambling to secure the future of the winter festival's cornerstone event. But thanks to a solid snow base and sponsorship, the three-day race that begins in downtown Anchorage is scheduled to run Feb. 23-25, sa…

title: First Dude fired up for Iron Dog
summary: When Todd Palin looks out the window of the governor's mansion in downtown Juneau, he often sees plenty of snow and even the occasional trailer topped with snowmobiles driving by. But Palin, a three-time champion of the Tesoro Iron Dog snowmachine race that begins Sunday on Big Lake, has done no riding in the capital city since wife Sarah Palin was elected governor. "I see a lot of snowmachines in Juneau," Palin said. "But it's a lot of mountain riding. I'm a flatlander." Iron Doggers generally ride thousands of miles to test their machines and hone their bodies for the 2,000-mile race, billed as the world's longest and toughest. For the second straight year, the course stretches from Big Lake to Nome to Fairbanks. Twenty-nine teams will chase a purse of nearly $125,000. But for Palin, jug…

title: The smart moose now know to look both ways
summary: Once again, the Alaska Department of Fish and Game is pleading with Anchorage motorists to avoid running into moose foolish enough to venture onto local streets. Is this warning really necessary? Isn't one of the givens of driving that you're not supposed to run into things? Not other cars. Not pedestrians. Not stops signs. Not buildings. Not moose, which stand 6 feet tall or better, weigh up to 1,000 pounds and can make a mess of your vehicle. Run down a pedestrian or bicyclist and you'll probably just scratch the paint and get blood all over your ride. But hit a moose, and you're likely to cave in the whole grill, take out the windshield, maybe even flatten the roof and smash the dash if you're in one of those little cars favored by what California Gov. Arnold Schwarzenegger might call "…

summary: Outdoors hot line Published: February 11, 2007 Last Modified: February 11, 2007 at 05:44 AM ASTRONOMY Program focuses on Pluto's demotion from planet status Learn why Pluto is no longer considered a planet at 7 p.m. Friday at the Eagle River Nature Center. . Bring binoculars or telescopes to share, and dress warmly. Due to space constraints, this program cannot accommodate large groups. Children can earn their Junior Astronomer Certificate after attending six programs and completing "Star Notes." Free, but parking is $5 for nonmembers. (694-2108) Event a fundraiser but chance to view sky too Spend a night with the stars that benefits the Eagle River Nature Center 6-9 p.m. Saturday at the center, at the end of Eagle River Road. title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Cellular cooperation could spare lives COMMUNICATION: Help arrives faster when a user's location can be tracked. By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: February 11, 2007 Last Modified: February 11, 2007 at 05:44 AM Better communication between cellular phone companies working in the north and the Alaska State Troopers might have spared a pair of lost Wasilla snowmobilers from a cold night in the Talkeetna Mountains in January, according to some involved in a two-day search near Eureka Summit earlier this year. More than 24 hours after Thomas Quirk called home on a cell phone to report he was lost, a trooper helicopter found the 43-year-old man and his 17-year-old son, Christopher. They were cold and shaken but safe in a cabin about 10 miles from the Eureka Lodge, a snowmobile hotb…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Kenai River still sports red salmon HEAD SOUTH: Flooding rivers in the Mat-Su Borough mean the good fishing is on the Kenai Peninsula. By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: August 24, 2006 Last Modified: August 24, 2006 at 04:51 AM Ship Creek silver salmon were snug up along the banks where the drag of the shoreline slowed the raging current when Alaska Department of Fish and Game biologist Dan Bosch looked for them this week. All around the region, unfortunately, the situation was similar. Where the salmon weren't fighting to stay out of the forest, as along badly flooded Willow Creek, they were skittering around invisible beneath high and turbid waters. The sole, significant exception appeared to be on the well-buffered Kenai River system where two huge lakes -- Kenai and Skila…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: The pace of competitive skating is set to quicken in Alaska By MELISSA DeVAUGHN Anchorage Daily News Published: February 4, 2007 Last Modified: February 4, 2007 at 03:38 AM On the near side of Westchester Lagoon on a recent Saturday, children and adults wobbled about on the ice, listening to music and occasionally stopping by the burn barrels to warm up. They were dressed for the weather, in insulated Carhartts, thick snow pants, heavy mittens and woolen caps. But just a few hundred yards away, a different scene unfolded. Athletes wearing shockingly thin but colorful unitards bent into the breeze, gliding along an ice oval prepared just for them. They looked like giant bullets in red, blue and yellow. And they were fast. Very fast. Unlike the skaters slipping and sliding, these folks swaye…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: CRAIG MEDRED At 20 below, remember: Even paradise gets boring CRAIG MEDRED OUTDOORS Published: February 4, 2007 Last Modified: February 4, 2007 at 03:36 AM On the day the wind hit 82 mph on the Hillside to create a snowstorm parallel to the ground, Kent Kaiser called from Down Under on a phone line so clear he could have been in downtown Anchorage. Though it was clear this unsolicited call from New Zealand was nothing more than an attempt to promote his guiding business there, it was hard to avoid the thought that maybe the former Alaskan was just calling to torment. He noted how his old hometown of Fairbanks had kicked off the New Year by grabbing for the 50-below-zero mark, though the temperature only got down to minus 44. Har, har, har. On New Zealand's South Island, he added, the tempe…
